data.title: Give Your Retirement The Ultimate Gift Of Income This Christmas
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

It is Christmas Eve! This is a season of joy, cheer, and time spent with family and loved ones.

Everyone and every family has their Christmas tradition that they follow year after year. Whether it is decorating the trees, setting up the lights, organizing a gathering, attending Church, giving to charity, or pursuing activities like baking cookies, watching specific movies, not to mention Santa's visits. While some traditions are widely followed, others are more closely adopted by a few. Did you know that in Japan, KFC fried chicken buckets are a massive tradition, followed by millions since the 70s? Whatever they may be, these rituals have one purpose – to create a sense of continuity and comfort that has little to do with the latest trend or passing fad.

Companies have their own way of participating in all the festivities through their own traditions. Year after year, they put up ad campaigns to attract customers. They sell you those ornaments, decorations, lights, cookies, candy, and gift baskets, and participate in all the Merriment. Some maintain a tradition of paying bonuses to their employees.

Similarly, dividends are a tradition that has been adopted for centuries by companies of all sizes. And dividend-paying companies operate in much the same way. They don’t need to chase the hottest thing or reinvent themselves every cycle. Instead, they focus on generating steady cash flows, protecting their balance sheets, and regularly sharing excess profits with shareholders – quarter after quarter, year after year. Much like your trusted holiday tradition, their appeal isn’t in surprises, but in consistency.

In that spirit, let’s look at two income picks that reflect the enduring value of consistency.

Pick #1: Verizon – Yield 6.9%

Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) is the largest wireless operator in the United States, with 146.1 million subscribers at the end of the third quarter. The market continues to underappreciate VZ due to its higher debt and a perception of low to non-existent growth in the business. Yet, over the years, the company has generated strong and predictable FCF (Free Cash Flow) that adequately covers its dividend payments. This was also the case in 2022, which was the company’s peak CapEx year, with $23.1 billion spent on 5G and fiber expansion.

Speaking of debt, VZ ended Q3 with a modest Net debt to Adj. EBITDA of 2.2x, and the company maintains an investment-grade A- balance sheet. YTD, VZ has paid $8.6 billion in dividends to shareholders, while generating $15.8 billion in FCF. With CapEx continuing to reduce in the years ahead, VZ is well-positioned to grow its FCF and support further growth in its dividends.

VZ has delivered annual dividend raises for 19 years, and its current yield represents a 6.9% yield. The company maintained its FY 2025 guidance, and expects 2.5% to 3.5% EBITDA growth and 1-3% EPS growth for the year. VZ expects FCF between $19.5-$20.5 billion, placing its annual dividend at a modest 57% payout ratio.

The Internet is the lifeblood of the digital economy. Without reliable internet connectivity, even the most state-of-the-art data center out there will be of no use. VZ is one of three companies that can operate nationwide, delivering necessary services to millions of customers and steady dividends to shareholders.

Pick #2: SLRC – Yield 10.8%

Middle market companies can’t borrow efficiently from traditional lenders, and VCs only want businesses that can produce massive growth over the years of losing money to grab market share. What about those businesses that are slower growers, but consistently profitable?

There are BDCs (Business Development Companies). While most BDCs focus on loans backed by the cash flows of their borrowers, SLR Capital Corporation (SLRC) is unique in its significant and specialized focus on Asset-Based Lending and equipment financing, which collectively represent 76% of the BDC’s loan portfolio.

As of September 30, 2025, 98.2% of SLRC’s portfolio comprised first-lien senior secured loans, and the BDC boasts strong due diligence and underwriting skills in its niche focus areas, as seen from the fact that 99.7% of its portfolio was performing as expected, with only one loan ($5.8 million at fair value) in non-accrual status. It is worth noting that SLRC did come across First Brands as an investment opportunity, but according to Co-CEO Bruce Spohler, they rejected it due to several red flags that surfaced during their due diligence processes.

Despite fears around the private credit sector, SLRC delivered book value growth during the third quarter, with NAV at $18.21/share. The BDC’s net portfolio activity was its strongest ever in the ABL segment at $57.5 million, having originated $301.6 million in loans during the third quarter.

SLRC maintains an investment-grade BBB- rating by Fitch, and ended Q3 with leverage at 1.13x – at the midpoint of its target range of 0.9x to 1.25x net debt to equity. Management noted during the Q3 conference call that they have over $850 million in capital available to deploy.

As borrowers continue to seek liquidity in a capital-starved environment, BDCs rise to the occasion, albeit selectively, to cater to an underserved market. SRLC's business is focused on extending loans, and the BDC has a strong track record of underwriting discipline and has delivered steady dividend payments over the years. This private-credit focused security has significantly outperformed a passive high-yield corporate bond ETF since its inception.

SLRC’s $0.41/share quarterly dividend calculates to a 10.8% annualized yield.

Conclusion

The best Christmas traditions aren’t flashy. They don’t need to be reinvented each year, and their charm doesn’t fade over time. In fact, their strength lies in repetition, because they provide a quiet assurance of consistency, no matter what the world is going through at the moment.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we embrace the same principles and keep market noise away from our financial and mental well-being by ensuring that our well-diversified portfolio keeps producing consistent paychecks through dividends and interest payments across market cycles. Today’s picks are just two of over 45 holdings that work together to deliver consistent income and support true financial freedom.

With that, we wish all our readers a Merry Christmas to those who celebrate, and Happy Holidays to everyone. May the season bring peace, joy, and the comfort that comes from knowing some things, like great traditions and reliable income, are built to last.

data.title: 2026 Market Outlook: Heading Towards Major Crossroads
data.description:
The stock market is about to wrap up another year of strong gains due to the surge in AI sector stocks. The market is at a crossroads with premium valuations and a big 3-year run reducing the likelihood of further major upside while opening up downside risk in the next year. The Fed has cut interest rates to end 2025 and the tailwind of more rates cuts with a new Fed Chief support higher stock prices in 2026, but the market faces elevated inflation risks that could undercut any rally.

The initial S&P 500 target for 2026 is 7,900, though another year of big gains could tip inflation into requiring the Fed to adjust monetary policy, impacting the bull market.

Market Pressing Higher

As of December 18, the S&P 500 (SPY, SPX) has already generated a total return of 18.4%. The benchmark index has now hit 3 consecutive years of strong returns following the big 18% loss in 2022.

Just as important, the stock market reported a loss back in 2018 and 2020 started off with a huge dip due to the Covid shutdowns. The S&P 500 definitely hasn't seen runaway returns this decade and especially since 2018 with 2 full years with losses, but the positive years have been really good with all 6 positive years since 2018 having returns in excess of 18.4%.

On the flip side, the large market returns since 2008 with just 2 years with losses and only 2022 with a material loss topping 10% has left the stock market extremely over valued. The S&P 500 trades at near the highest forward P/E multiple in history at 23x forward EPS estimates.

Going back to 2000, the S&P 400 MidCap and S&P 500 SmallCap indexes are much more reasonably valued. Both indexes of smaller stocks trade closer to 16x earnings versus the Magnificent-7 stock trading at over 28x forward earnings.

The Russell 2000 (IWM) trades at 18x current EPS targets. The index of small cap stocks trades near the peak multiple over the last 40 years with the Russell 2000 constantly topping out just under 20x.

The index was a much better buy at the lows with the Russell 2000 trading at levels below 12x forward EPS target. The iShares Russell 2000 ETF reached $40 in 2009, down 50% from the 2000 peak. The index also provided great buying points in 2023 and other entry points over the last nearly 20 years when the market dipped.

The point here is that the S&P 500 and Russell 2000 indexes can clearly continue running higher. The indexes trading at premium forward P/E multiples doesn't predict an immediate stock market collapse, but it doesn't provide the opportune time to build a position. Under the surface, the smaller stocks and value plays offer much better value.

Don't Fight The Fed

Back on December 10, the Fed cut interest rates by 25 basis points to a current level of 3.50% to 3.75% for the 3rd rate cut this year. Naturally, these rate cuts take 6-12 months to boost the economy and the first cut was only back on September 18.

The market is predicting another 2 rate cuts in 2026 while the Fed Fund Rate futures predicting an interest rate of 3.08%. According to the CME, the market is predicting anywhere from 1 to 3 rate cuts in the next 12 months for a 2026 ending target rate of 3.0% to 3.25%.

U.S. inflation levels aren't very conducive for lower interest rates next year. Truflation regularly calculated inflation below 2% until a recent bump in the last couple of months has the reported rate up at 2.28%, possibly signaling the recent rate cuts along with tariffs have pressured prices higher.

This modern US inflation calculator uses millions of real-prices data points to calculate inflation. The BLS CPI inflation was last reported at 2.7% for November with the core CPI at 2.6% for the month after the BLS didn't report a number for October due to the government shutdown. The CPI actually dipped to 2.3% back in April and has risen in the last 7 months, which isn't very supportive of the Fed cutting interest rates.

The inflation metrics came done from the 3.0% levels for both headline and core inflation when the BLS last reported metrics for September. CPI inflation continues to trend generally in the wrong direction with the inflation levels still above the Fed's preference for a 2% target.

A big market upset in 2026 would be continued inflation pressure due in part to the 3 rate cuts in late 2025 boosting the economy in 2026. The Fed getting back to a point of needing to fight inflation with core CPI reaching 3% would be very problematic for a stock market trading at historically rich valuations.

President Trump continues to press for lower interest rates and is set to announce a new Fed Chairman with Powell's term ending on May 15, 2026. The setup suggests a few quick rate cuts, but also a scenario where any contribution to higher inflation leaves the Fed in a pickle with a President pressing for rate cuts and the data not supporting further cuts.

S&P 500 Price Target

The stock market is due for a more normal market return in the 10% range, versus the run of roughly 20% annual gains in the 2020s. The consensus analyst estimates forecast record earnings next year at $312, up nearly 15% from an estimated $272 for 2025.

The S&P 500 would reach 7,935 based on matching the forecasted 15% EPS growth for the benchmark index. The real risk is for the benchmark index to sprint higher before ultimately reversing on a sudden change in the Fed rate cuts described above due to stock market gains contributing to higher inflation.

The consensus Wall St. forecasts for the S&P 500 sit around 7,500, perfect for the typical 8% to 10% annual market gain. Deutsche Bank targets a 15% gain to 8,000 while Bank of America is only predicting a 4% return with none of the analysts predicting a down year.

The stock market doesn't usually trade up in such predictable ranges. The more likely outcome is a year of strong gains boosted by 1 to 2 additional rate cuts eventually interrupted by higher inflation and the need to hike interest rates. Whether this actually occurs in 2026 or 2027 is difficult to predict, but the market will soon hit a level of 4 to 5 years of big gains and rich valuations limiting further upside with rate hikes ultimately halting the bull market.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that the market is likely still in a bullish mode this year with the Fed cutting interest rates, pressured by President Trump. Though, the major risk towards the end of the year is 4 years of strong stock market results pushing the Fed tailwinds towards disappearing going into 2027. After all, the Fed historically doesn't cut interest rates in the face of a very healthy stock market.

data.title: Ares Capital Vs. Blue Owl Capital: A Battle Of BDC Titans
data.description:
Investment thesis

Ares Capital (ARCC) and Blue Owl Capital Corp (OBDC) are two heavyweights in the world of Business Development Companies (BDC), considered "blue chips" in the sector thanks to their size and long history. There is intense competition between these companies for clients in the Upper Middle Market segment. They share a fundamental similarity in their strategic focus, taking a conservative approach to portfolio formation. Both boast a huge capital base and access to high-quality deals. This is thanks to ARCC's parent company, Ares Management, and OBDC's access to the Blue Owl platform. Nevertheless, it is possible to find significant differences between them, due to which some investors may choose ARCC, while others may choose OBDC. The results of fundamental analysis reveal not only the fundamental differences between these companies, as well as their differing assessments of overall profitability.

Fundamental similarities and differences between ARCC and OBDC

From my introduction, you can see that ARCC and OBDC are essentially the main leaders in the BDC sector, following a traditional strategy of financing innovative companies, albeit only those with more significant EBITDA. This metric averages around $170 million for companies in ARCC's portfolio and around $200 million for those in OBDC's. Thanks to their size and origins in financial giants (Ares Management and Blue Owl), both ARCC and OBDC have access to significant amounts of financing, enabling them to execute large deals that are not available to other BDC players (those focused on small and medium-sized businesses or venture lending).

The lending policies of ARCC and OBDC are built around partnerships with other private investment funds, who offer them as an additional source of financing to their clients. This is a group of companies with stable businesses, with more consistent financial performance and core business results. Thanks to this, risks of non-performing loans remain low, allowing ARCC and OBDC to maintain a high-quality loan portfolio.

Despite these similarities, there are a number of differences between ARCC and OBDC that are important to mention. First and foremost, these companies have different approaches to building their investment portfolios. In essence, they have different risk appetites. While Ares focuses on total return metrics, making it historically more aggressive and less concentrated in first lien, the OBDC has a defensive strategy that allows it to position itself defensively within the BDC sector.

One of the distinctive features of ARCC's strategy is the fact that a significant portion of its portfolio is concentrated in the Second Lien and Preferred Equity segments. Furthermore, the ARCC managers have built up a significant amount of common stock in client companies, allowing them to receive additional income during certain periods, to be used for the payment of special dividends (the last time this happened was in 2022). Even though ARCC's strategy is more aggressive, the portfolio is pretty diversified, covering 587 companies. Because of this, there's less risk of a single default. Only 12% of the capital is concentrated in the top 10 borrowers, compared to about 19% for OBDC.

Nevertheless, the same Blue Owl can boast that approximately 74% of its portfolio is concentrated in First Lien Senior Secured Loans. As a result, if a borrower defaults, it is OBDC that will be first in line to receive repayment after the sale of the distressed debtor's assets and property. In addition, although it has fewer assets in its portfolio, the OBDC strategy is more focused on individual players.

But I want to take a closer look at the difference between ARCC and OBDC through a comparison table based on key criteria. For me, one important evaluation criterion is total return. Total return for the last year shows ARCC's advantage with a return of +3.2%, whereas OBDC has a negative return of -6.98%. And this is despite the fact that OBDC's forward dividend yield is 2.7% higher. These dynamics are due to other differences between the companies, on which I will focus in more detail in the next section.

Comparing key indicators between ARCC and OBDC, we can see once again that Blue Owl has more secure loans due to its huge share of First Lien. Moreover, the dividend yield is higher at 12.33%, whereas Ares' is 9.63%. Nevertheless, the dividend coverage ratio is 116.6% (due to the payment of a special dividend), whereas ARCC has greater resilience. Therefore, given the potential economic downturn, ARCC's dividend payments are more secure, whereas OBDC's payments are more vulnerable to potential reductions. The last time this happened was three years ago.

ARCC's premium status is reflected in the fact that its shares are often traded at a premium to NAV. OBDC's shares are often traded at or slightly below par value. This is an interesting investment for investors who are focused not only on income but also on capital appreciation. But if the P/NAV ratio for ARCC shares falls to 1.05x or lower, it can be considered an excellent time to enter a position.

Criterion

66.36%

Reasons for choosing ARCC for my investment portfolio

Based on the analysis above, I would prefer ARCC. That's because it strikes a good balance between returns and risks. However, I've got two main reasons for choosing Ares for my investment portfolio. Firstly, ARCC has seen modest growth in non-performing loans, whereas OBDC has seen a slight increase in debt on which interest accrual has been suspended.

Take, as an example, key metrics such as non-accruals in relation to fair value and cost. At ARCC, these indicators will be 1.0% and 1.8%, having decreased by 0.2% each in Q3 2025 compared to Q2 2025. Median industry fair value is currently nearly 4%. This ratio of non-performing debt is below the company's historical average (approximately 2.8%). Decrease in the share of non-performing loans in Q3 2025 is attributed to the company's debt restructuring efforts. A prime example is the case involving Potomac Energy Center, in which Ares managed to turn a profit after all.

The OBDC saw a reversal in Q3 2025. These indicators grew rather than declined. The fair value ratio, however, rose from 0.7% to 1.3%. The report says the Beauty Industry Group case is why problem debt is up. But this figure is still low, particularly compared to the industry median. Right now, the market is too sensitive, and investors are nervous. That's why this case still affected OBDC's share price, causing it to drop a bit.

Another reason for picking ARCC is that it's more sensitive to interest rates than OBDC. These two companies have a big chunk of floating-rate loans (96% and 97%, respectively). In order to assess how a 50 basis point cut in the Fed rate will affect them, let's look at the information that ARCC and OBDC provide in their 10-Q reports.

Calculation of sensitivity for ARCC. According to its financial statements, a 100 basis point reduction in the Fed rate would result in a decrease in interest income of $0.19 per share (per year). Taking a 50 basis point reduction in the rate, it would be $0.095 per share. Its current interest income per share is $0.50. That means a 0.5% rate cut would bring income down to $0.476 (per quarter). Given that the regular dividend is $0.48, the ARCC would still cover its payments, albeit without the same margin of safety.

Calculation of sensitivity for OBDC. According to its financial statements, if the Fed cuts interest rates by 100 basis points, interest income will go down by $0.22 per share (per year). If the rate is cut by 50 basis points, the drop will be $0.11 per share. Current interest income per share is $0.36. That means a 0.5% rate cut would lead to a drop in income to $0.332 (per quarter). Given that the regular dividend is $0.37, the OBDC will essentially have to either reduce its payments or use its accumulated capital.

Final results of calculations of companies' sensitivity to a 50 basis point cut in the Fed rate are shown in the table below.

Criterion

ARCC

OBDC

Decrease in NII per share (year)

-$0.024

Forecast NII

Change of coverage

The safety margin will disappear, but the coating will remain

Coverage will be insufficient, potentially leading to a reduction in dividends

Risks of the investment thesis

There is no denying that the economic environment of the BDC sector is dependent on interest rates, since most capital loans have floating rates. Given that the Federal Reserve continues to pursue a monetary policy of lowering key rates, there is an expected increase in interest rate risk for companies such as ARCC and OBDC. The negative impact of easing conditions in the money market may be short-term, as the business and financial climate is expected to improve in the medium to long term. Furthermore, in assessing the impact of interest rate sensitivity, our calculations have determined that ARCC has a significantly higher margin of safety than OBDC. As such, these risks are less significant for investments in Ares than for Blue Owl.

Conclusion

Based on my analysis of companies such as ARCC and OBDC, I have determined that ARCC shares are the most attractive investment during the Fed's monetary policy easing period, because their portfolio and financial profile are better prepared for continued interest rate cuts. In this regard, I recommend ARCC as a "Buy." Since OBDC may potentially reduce its regular dividends or cancel its special dividends, my recommendation for OBDC is to "Hold." Furthermore, the total return results for the past year show that ARCC is a more accomplished player in large deals in the BDC market than Blue Owl. The latter can boast of its high dividend yield and more secure loans due to its investments in Second Lien. But this is not enough for the market, resulting in a loss on OBDC shares in 2025.

data.title: MSTU Exposes Investors To More Leverage Than Expected
data.description:
The T-Rex 2X Long MSTR Daily Target ETF (MSTU) is a leveraged exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with 2x the daily performance of Strategy (MSTR) shares. MSTU can be utilized by traders seeking to amplify their exposure to MSTR shares, though may be exposed to substantial volatility in doing so, making this strategy only appropriate for seasoned traders with strict risk management. Given the risks associated with the underlying MSTR shares, I am recommending MSTU with a Sell rating.

MSTU should only be considered for daily exposure; investing in MSTU for longer than a single trading day may expose investors to significant risks, including compounded returns, value decay, and unrecoverable losses.

About T-Rex 2X Long MSTR Daily Target ETF

MSTU was launched by Rex Shares on September 18, 2024 on the CBOE Exchange. MSTU was designed to provide traders with 2x the daily performance of MSTR shares through the use of total return swaps [TRSs]. Using the TRSs, MSTU has the ability to leverage exposure to achieve the 2x daily performance target.

MSTU takes both the payer and receiver position across its strategy, balancing exposure across both ends of the trade. As the receiver, MSTU will receive total return exposure to MSTR shares’ performance in exchange for a fixed rate. As the payer, MSTU will receive a fixed rate in exchange for MSTR shares’ performance. Swaps are generally settled daily in cash, exchanging the daily performance for the fixed rate.

MSTU shares have been under significant pressure in recent months as Strategy’s financial position weakens. The underlying cause of the financial challenges is the declining price of bitcoin. This has directly impacted Strategy given its leveraged bitcoin strategy, in which Strategy would issue debt in order to purchase additional bitcoin to be held in the treasury. Though the strategy has gained interest by retail investors as an alternative bitcoin investment vehicle, the strategy has since faced hardship with the price of bitcoin declining by -8% in the last year, which has pressured MSTR shares to decline by -50%.

As a result of the significant selling in the last year, MSTU shares have declined by over -91%. For long-term investors, the price decline may have led to significant value decay and unsalvageable losses.

As a result of the price decline in bitcoin, Strategy has been increasing its liquidity in order to remain in good standing for its preferred shares and debt. Accordingly, Strategy has increased its cash position to $2.19b. As of q3’25, Strategy had $8.2b in total debt on the balance sheet with a financial leverage ratio of 1.40x assets/equity. I believe the biggest challenge presented is that Strategy used conventional debt in order to gain leveraged bitcoin exposure as compared to using TRSs like leveraged funds. The critical challenge is that Strategy is investing in non-interest-bearing assets using debt with the intention of employing a buy-and-hold strategy on the bitcoin purchased. This means that Strategy will need to raise cash in order to cover interest and dividend payments on debt and preferred shares, adding certain risks to its leveraged bitcoin holdings, particularly during periods of market decline.

MSTU shares have been reflective of the significant price decline faced by MSTR shares at an amplified rate. Looking at daily performance, MSTU shares may be set for further decline relative to MSTR shares.

Despite the generally positive fund flows into MSTU, shares have faced a sharp decline as a result of pricing, resulting in a -$2b change in net assets in the last year.

Taking into consideration fund flows in the last few months, investors may be expecting a reversal in the underlying share price. The challenge presented is that MSTR faces a number of factors that may influence the share price, including the price of bitcoin, remaining in good standing on its debt, and the possibility of MSTR being delisted from MSCI indices, all of which may pressure the share price.

Risks Related to MSTU

MSTU is a leveraged ETF designed to provide traders with 2x the daily performance of MSTR shares, exposing traders to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. MSTU may expose investors to substantial risk related to the price of bitcoin, particularly when considering MSTR shares as a leveraged bitcoin strategy. MSTU employs leverage that may result in enhanced exposure to MSTR shares and may result in compounded returns, value decay, and significant losses if held for longer than a single trading day. Actively trading MSTU may expose investors to higher capital gains taxes, trading fees, and management fees; MSTU charges a 105bps expense ratio and has an average bid/ask spread of 0.11%.

You can review additional risks here and the SEC bulletin here.

Given the substantial risks associated with trading leveraged funds, investors should adhere to strict risk management policies, particularly when it comes to sell discipline.

Final Thoughts

MSTR shares may face additional selling pressure with operational and stock-related catalysts coming into play, particularly related to making interest and dividend payments on its debt and preferred shares, the price of bitcoin, and the possibility of being delisted from MSCI indices. Given these risks, as well as the substantial exposure gained while trading leveraged funds, I am recommending MSTU with a Sell rating.

data.title: Buy The Dip: 2 Of The Best Undervalued 8%-10% Yields I've Seen
data.description:
While the market is priced at very frothy levels, and tech stocks are in favor while dividend stocks are largely out of favor, as the massive outperformance of the S&P 500 (SPY) compared to the swap-use-dividend-equity ETF (SCHD) over the past three years demonstrates, I am finding very attractive contrarian opportunities in the high-yielding infrastructure and credit sectors.

In this article, I’m going to detail two opportunities that yield between 8% and 10% that I think are undervalued right now and offer asymmetric upside relative to the risks they face moving forward.

An 8%-Yielding Midstream Giant With Improving Capital Discipline

The first opportunity I am going to talk about is Energy Transfer (ET). It issues a K1 tax form, so if that is not for you, you can either invest in an MLP ETF that issues a 1099 tax form, like the GlobalX MLP ETF (MLPA) or the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP), or you can skip on to the next opportunity in this article, which issues a 1099 tax form.

ET is a primarily fee-based, midstream infrastructure business with one of the most diversified asset portfolios in North America. Its cash flows are largely commodity price resistant, as 90% of its EBITDA comes from fee-based contracts, while its portfolio is well diversified by basin and commodity type, as it has natural gas, NGL, crude, terminal, and storage.

This gives it significant cash flow stability due to its fee-based structure as well as its diversification. It also means that if any single business line or basin falls on hard times, the rest of the business model should help to carry the burden and keep the company from suffering any catastrophic declines in cash flow.

Additionally, it supports stable distributable cash flow growth, even in flat-to-weak commodity pricing environments, especially because the company retains so much distributable cash flow that it can reinvest in its substantial growth pipeline. The company is also benefiting from natural gas demand tailwinds, as it has already signed several deals to supply natural gas to Oracle data centers.

Another thing I like about ET a lot right now is that management is showing increasing capital discipline. In the past, they pursued growth projects that proved to be quite costly in terms of headaches and expenses for management, with cost overruns and major project delays. However, they recently suspended their Lake Charles LNG project because they believe the LNG market is currently a bit oversupplied. Additionally, ET sees itself primarily as a pipeline operator and, therefore, is hesitant to move outside of its circle of competence. They also wanted to sell at least 80% of the equity in the project in order to reduce their exposure to it.

Instead, they’re going to be steering that capital towards their Trans-Western Desert Southwest expansion project that is fully contracted, is long-dated, and has investment-grade counterparties. This is much lower execution risk, faster cash flow conversion, and therefore has higher certainty of returns versus their mega LNG project.

The company also has a very attractive and well-covered distribution with a forward yield of over 8% and a coverage ratio of nearly two times on a distributable cash flow basis. Additionally, their leverage ratio is on the low end of their 4.0 to 4.5 times target range, which gives them substantial capital flexibility. As such, they’re able to reinvest quite aggressively into their substantial growth pipeline, which, when combined with a strong balance sheet and substantial distributable cash flow coverage ratio of their distribution, enables them to grow their distribution at a 3-5% annualized pace.

Moreover, their EBITDA multiple of just 7.76 times is well below their 10-year average of 9.11 times and is also well below peers like MPLX (MPLX) and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), which makes them appear to be one of the best values in the midstream space today.

When you combine their yield of over 8% with a mid-single-digit expected annualized distribution CAGR, they have a path to generating low- to mid-teens annualized total returns, especially when you factor in the potential for valuation multiple expansion, which could push that total return profile even higher.

A 10%+ Yielding BDC Trading Below Its Private Market Value

The other opportunity I’m going to discuss is a business development company (BIZD) called Fidus Investment (FDUS). FDUS is one of the more conservatively run BDCs that invests in 82.8% 1st and 2nd lien secured debt while also investing in equity opportunities that contribute to long-term NAV growth and overall upside for the company, in addition to the attractive yield it pays out. In fact, its NAV per share has been above its IPO level for 57 consecutive quarters, showing the strength of its underwriting discipline and its ability to generate growth through its common equity investments.

Additionally, its underwriting excellence is demonstrated by its non-accruals that stand at just 0.3% of the portfolio's fair value, putting it among the lowest in the sector.

Moreover, with only 50 basis points of Fed reserve rate cuts expected this next year, and that number likely potentially seeing downside given the recent strong GDP growth and yet elevated inflation numbers, that helps FDUS support its income stream, since most of its investments are in floating-rate loans that are indexed to short-term interest rates.

On top of that, FDUS's base dividend coverage ratio is strong, with $0.50 per share Q3 adjusted NII, while its base dividend was only $0.43 per share. This puts its coverage ratio at 86%, which is fairly conservative for a business development company and gives FDUS the flexibility to pay out regular supplemental dividends to further boost its yield.

In fact, FDUS's base yield now is at 9.2%, and its total yield is over 10% when you include its supplemental distributions. Not only that, but while it has historically traded at a premium to NAV, FDUS currently trades at a 4% discount to NAV, meaning that this is an opportunity to buy one of the highest quality BDCs at a discount to its private market value, as well as an even bigger discount to its historic valuation multiple.

Risks To Watch & Investor Takeaway

Of course, no investment is risk-free, and neither are ET and FDUS. ET continues to face significant execution risk that includes regulatory delays and cost overruns on its huge projects that could pop up and derail its growth momentum. Additionally, while it has only 10% EBITDA exposure to commodity price volatility, if commodity prices plunge, ET’s unit price will likely fall in tandem, even if its cash flows hold up fairly well.

Meanwhile, FDUS is interest rate-sensitive, so if the Fed does cut rates more rapidly than expected, it will weigh on its NII per share quite substantially and potentially eventually threaten its base dividend, and we do not expect this for the foreseeable future. Additionally, its significant exposure to equity in lower-middle market companies means that if the economy were to fall into a deep recession, FDUS could see a meaningful uptick in non-accruals that could weaken its net asset value, as well as its NII per share, and also weigh on market sentiment, thereby pushing its price-to-NAV ratio even lower.

However, with the economy appearing to be on sound footing for now at least, and given FDUS’s track record, as well as ET’s diversified, stable cash flow profile and significant retained cash flow, as well as both of their attractive yields and valuation multiples, the risk-reward appears to be quite attractive with both.

Therefore, I rate them both as attractive buys right now, and they are at the very top of my watchlist for potential inclusion in my portfolios at High Yield Investor, where I will notify members as soon as I add them to my portfolios.

Let me know in the comments below which of these two high-yields you prefer and think is a better buy right now.

data.title: CytomX Therapeutics: EpCAM Is Back, Now Prove It Holds (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

My last look at CytomX Therapeutics, Inc. (CTMX) was in October and followed preliminary data for its EpCAM-targeted, topo-1 Probody-ADC, CX-2051.

The company is evaluating 2051 in metastatic colorectal cancer, or mCRC. Historically, ADCs have had a tough go in CRC. EpCAM, in particular, has been a challenging target because it runs into normal tissue toxicity. What CytomX has done is that it added a “probody mask” that is designed to reduce binding to normal tissues until the drug reaches tumor tissue.

While 2051 has, so far, avoided apparent EpCAM normal tissue toxicity (as evidenced by the absence of pancreatitis and liver toxicity), severe diarrhea, which is attributed to the payload class effect (topo 1), became a major focus after a Grade 5 (death) treatment-related acute kidney injury that appears to have resulted from severe diarrhea (leading to dehydration).

Looking ahead to Phase 1 expansion data in 1Q26, I proposed that CTMX merited a “speculative Buy” with limited allocation. Its stock has since risen by 28%.

The article that follows takes a closer look at the company ahead of major catalysts.

Recent Developments

Let’s take a step back and acknowledge what CytomX is tasked with when it comes to 2051. First, the EpCAM-directed ADC must achieve late-line CRC response rates clearly above the single-digit response rates typical for current late-line standards. Of course, durability is a must, too. Second, the probody mask must avoid the characteristic systemic EpCAM-driven toxicities that previously upended the target.

Regarding efficacy, not much has changed since my October write-up. Management continues to anchor to the May 2025 interim dataset and frames it as a substantial step up versus late-line CRC.

Across the 3 relevant dose levels that we're currently expanding, the 7.2, 8.6, and 10 mg/kg doses, we saw an integrated confirmed response rate of 28%, which very substantially beats the current standard of care in the late-line setting, where, as you know, response rates are in the single digits.

As for 1Q26 updates, the dataset is expected to move from pooled, small numbers into dose-separated efficacy and durability. This effort is based on the FDA’s Project Optimus, which aims to characterize doses and schedules of ADCs and other oncology therapies, “before initiating registration trials.”

Probably owing to limited options in later-line mCRC and the new mechanism, enrollment is accelerating faster than anticipated. The company now expects total enrollment in the Phase 1 study to be ~100 patients by the time the 1Q26 update hits.

On the safety front, 2051’s limiting toxicity to date has looked more like a payload class problem than a clean EpCAM normal tissue binding one, which is encouraging. Still, diarrhea remains a major issue, and the company is clearly laser-focused on getting “ahead of it.” For instance, CytomX has discussed the use of prophylactic loperamide as a protocol-level intervention.

All of this being said, if one thing is for certain, it is that 2051 is still in the nascent stages of its development. When pressed on FDA alignment and registrational expectations, management punted communications “next year.” While 2051’s safety issue appears manageable enough to operationalize across sites, it is clearly not a solved problem, and the company is actively learning more about it.

Of course, another major unknown is 2051’s exact place in mCRC. The earlier the line, the higher the bar. Notably, management expects to start a Phase 1b combination study with bevacizumab (Avastin) in 1Q26. While this is a strategic hedge, it also signals that monotherapy may not be enough for earlier lines (e.g., 2L). It also increases execution complexity because tolerability may change with bevacizumab tagging along.

Meanwhile, CX-801 (PROBODY Interferon alpha-2b) is being positioned as “platform validation,” rather than a near-term value driver. 801’s first meaningful efficacy readout for the combination is not expected until the end of next year.

Financial Health

CytomX ended Q3 with $143.6 million in cash, cash equivalents, and investments.

Revenue remains bumpy and took a bit of a hit last quarter. Q3 revenue totaled $6 million. The company highlighted a “decrease in Moderna activities due to Moderna budget considerations.” Recall that outside of 2051 and 801 (its second lead asset), CytomX has a “fully partnered pipeline” composed of a few preclinical assets.

CytomX estimates its cash runway extends to the “second quarter of 2027.” So, this really places the pressure on 2051 to deliver in its 1Q26 update.

Final Words

As implied by volatile collaboration revenue, CytomX is increasingly a two-asset clinical company. With a runway extending only to 2Q27, its valuation hinges on 2051 outcome quality and 801 follow-through.

Regarding 2051, the 1Q26 update must answer durability and evaluability cleanly. Although my last update only guided for ORR expectations, the market is likely also expecting color on the duration of response distribution (e.g., time to response and whether responses deepen over time) and a clear accounting of who is excluded from efficacy (e.g., N treated, N response evaluable, reasons for non-evaluability). A drug that merely delivers a high ORR with short-lived responses is likely D.O.A. The narrative following the 1Q26 update should clearly reflect a registrational strategy. If not, I suspect that CytomX’s stock could take a meaningful hit due to an imminent "cash crunch."

Net-net, I see good reason to cut back a bit on CTMX (rating downgrade to “Hold”) ahead of the 2051 catalyst until there is further clarity on a registrational pathway for 2051 and a more robust cash runway.

data.title: Centerra Gold: The North American Pivot Is Mispriced
data.description:
I have a bullish thesis on Centerra Gold (CGAU) based upon both the high commodity price environment we are currently operating within and the structural transformation of the business model. We are seeing a company that has rebuilt its cash reserves to $562 million and initiated the restart of the Thompson Creek molybdenum mine, extended the life of Mount Milligan by a decade, and advanced the Goldfield project in Nevada. The market is currently valuing Centerra at a profound discount to its peers—trading at multiples that suggest a company in decline—essentially pricing the company as if it were a stagnant producer rather than a growth vehicle with a fully funded pipeline.

Latest Financials

While the company reported a third-quarter net income of $292.2 million, the headline number includes a non-cash impairment reversal of $193.5 million at the Goldfield project, and when the company’s adjusted net earnings are considered ($66.4 million, or $0.33 per share), a clear demonstration of strong underlying profitability from its core operations can be seen after adjusting for the one-time accounting impact.

More importantly, the generation of $98.7 million in free cash flow in the third quarter clearly represents the heart of the bullish thesis regarding Centerra’s future. The free cash flow was driven by the combination of the company’s strong operating performance (particularly at the Öksüt Mine in Turkey) and a very strong average realized gold price of $3,178 per ounce during the period. Even though the company incurred $32.6 million of non-sustaining capital expenditures associated with the restart of the Thompson Creek project during the period, the company was still able to increase its cash balances.

The company also reported a cash balance of $561.8 million and no debt outstanding. Including the unused corporate credit facility of $400 million, the company’s total liquidity is approximately $961 million. This represents a fortress balance sheet. At a time when most companies growing their business require additional financing either through secondary offerings or the issuance of expensive debt, Centerra has a unique advantage.

Operational costs continue to be a key component of success within the inflationary environment experienced across the globe today. The company’s consolidated gold production costs averaged $1,346 per ounce, and the all-in sustaining costs (on a by-product basis) averaged $1,652 per ounce. Although the company’s costs are reflective of the broader industry trends, the high margins earned on the sale of the company’s products (influenced primarily by the high level of copper production of 13.4 million pounds) provide the company with a high degree of flexibility in terms of generating profit relative to its cost structure.

Strategic Pivot: Mount Milligan’s Longevity

Centerra's valuation had an overarching cloud for many years. The cloud stemmed from investors' perception of a relatively short mine life for the core asset in the company's portfolio, Mount Milligan, located in the province of British Columbia. The recent announcement of the pre-feasibility study by Centerra in September 2025 essentially eliminated this cloud. Yet, investors have taken their time in fully adjusting Centerra's valuation to reflect this new reality.

The pre-feasibility study confirmed a mine life extension of Mount Milligan to 2045. In doing so, the company added a decade of visibility to its production profile in one of the premier jurisdictions globally. Of particular interest are the reserve updates for Mount Milligan. Proven and probable reserves at Mount Milligan were increased to 4.4 million ounces of gold (an increase of 56 percent) and 1.7 billion pounds of copper (an increase of 52 percent). This is far more than simply an incremental update of reserves; rather, it represents a fundamental shift in the asset's value proposition.

The Molybdenum Strategy: Thompson Creek

Gold-focused investors have long viewed the molybdenum segment of the business as a secondary consideration; however, the restart of the molybdenum-rich Thompson Creek Mine located in Idaho provides a unique opportunity to realize previously latent value at Centerra Gold Inc. The restart of the mine, with the decision to execute on the restart plan and produce molybdenum products in the second half of 2027, will essentially place a world-class asset back into a global supply-constrained molybdenum market.

The economic viability of the U.S. Moly (Thompson Creek Mine and the Langeloth Processing Facility) business is very good. The financials presented from the feasibility study indicate an after-tax net present value at an 8% discount rate of $472 million and an internal rate of return of 22%, both of which include the vertical integration of the mine with the Langeloth facility in Pennsylvania. As Centerra produces its own high-quality concentrate, it maximizes the total margin chain and reduces its dependence on potentially volatile and margin-squeezing third-party concentrate purchases.

The estimated capital expenditure of $397 million required to start the project is large; however, Centerra has spent or committed to spending approximately $113 million to date and has a strong cash position, providing ample funding to complete the project without the need to raise additional funds from the bond markets.

Goldfield and Kemess Growth Plan

In addition to the short-term revenue generated from Öksüt and Mount Milligan and the near-term restart of Thompson Creek, Centerra also has a long-term growth pipeline that will continue to grow through the 2030s. The first of these projects is the Goldfield Project, located in Nevada. A technical study was recently completed in August 2025 and outlined a project with an after-tax net present value at a 5% discount rate of $245 million and a strong 30% internal rate of return.

While Goldfield is not expected to be a large mine, with estimated peak annual production of approximately 100,000 ounces, it is expected to be a very high-return and low capital-intensity project ($252 million initial capital expenditure). In order to mitigate potential risks associated with this project, the management team has implemented a hedging program where they have committed to hedging 50% of their anticipated production in 2029 and 2030, using zero-cost collars with a floor price of $3,200 per ounce. This will ensure that regardless of whether gold prices fall or rise, the capital expenditures related to this project will be recovered.

The second of the longer-term projects that Centerra is working on is the Kemess Project, located in British Columbia. Centerra anticipates publishing a preliminary economic assessment on this project during the first quarter of 2026. The advantages of the Kemess Project include a vast amount of pre-existing infrastructure, including power lines and a camp, which historically reduce both the execution risk and capital requirements for mining operations.

Valuation Context

The disconnect between the market and the reality of Centerra Gold's financial situation can best be illustrated by the company's Enterprise Value to EBITDA ratio. On a TTM basis, Centerra Gold is currently trading at an EV/EBITDA of 5.52, which is approximately 49% lower than the sector median of 10.84. For the year ahead, the picture is even better. The forecasted EV/EBITDA multiple declines to 4.58 versus the sector median of 9.17. This type of discount is usually only seen with companies with catastrophic balance sheet issues or companies whose operations are failing, neither of which applies to Centerra Gold.

It is also important to evaluate cash flows since these are critical to support a self-funding model. The Price-to-Cash Flow for Centerra Gold is 8.66, or approximately 24% lower than the sector median of 11.46. Forecasted, this decreases to 8.07 versus the peer median of 10.54. As such, this metric is important to validate Centerra Gold's ability to fund the restart of the Thompson Creek Project and the Goldfield development from internal cash flows. The market is offering a cash flow yield that is significantly greater than the sector average, therefore providing a margin of safety for investors until the growth projects begin generating cash flows.

Counterarguments and Risk Analysis

Gold is presently experiencing a bull-run, however, Centerra is not exclusively a gold producer; it produces copper and molybdenum. The success of the planned restart of the Thompson Creek Mine is dependent on a strong global molybdenum market. Any decline in Chinese steel demand (and consequently the global demand for steel) could adversely affect the expected financial returns from Centerra's U.S. Moly Division. Additionally, although Centerra will benefit from the vertical integration between its U.S. Moly Division and its Langeloth smelter, the estimated net present value of this operation ($472M) is subject to fluctuations in metal pricing.

Presently, the Öksüt Mine in Turkey is the largest source of free cash flow for Centerra, producing $133.6 million in Q3 versus $44.6 million generated by Mount Milligan in Canada. Although the performance of Öksüt is excellent, it is situated in Turkey. Operations at Öksüt appear to be very stable, and the relationship between the Turkish Government and Centerra is apparently good (as evidenced by the sliding-scale royalty structure). However, any potential future regional political instability or changes in regulations could negatively impact Centerra's ability to generate the necessary funding for the North American pipeline.

The next twelve months will represent a period of "heavy-lifting" for Centerra. Centerra is planning to restart a mine (Thompson Creek) and commence construction on a new mine (Goldfield), while simultaneously embarking upon a major optimization effort at Mount Milligan. All of these projects present operational challenges. Capital blowouts are common in the mining industry. As such, while Centerra does possess a substantial amount of cash ($562 million), inflationary pressures on both labor and material costs in North America may negatively impact the expected financial returns from Centerra's operations. The current estimate for average all-in sustaining cost (AISC) is $1,650-$1,750/oz; any delays in project execution and/or any downward movement in gold prices could negatively affect the company's margin.

Future Outlook and Conclusion

The catalysts for Centerra Gold in 2026 include: The release of the Kemess preliminary economic assessment (PEA) in the first quarter of 2026; the continued development at the Goldfield Mine; and the optimization study for an extended life of mine (LOM) at the Öksüt Mine. The way I see it, investors are being paid for the current production as they receive the growth pipeline, the available cash, and the opportunity for the company to trade at a higher multiple than it does now without an increase in cost or a change in the company's operations.

data.title: Virtus KAR Mid-Cap Core Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Summary

Technology stocks—and artificial intelligence (AI)-related stocks in particular—continued to dominate headlines throughout the third quarter, providing a strong tailwind for the large-cap growth segment, which advanced 10.51%, as measured by the Russell 1000® Growth Index. Midway through August, small-cap stocks staged a notable surge with the Russell 2000® Index advancing 12.39%, its strongest quarterly performance since 2021. Small-cap performance was supported by growing expectations that the Federal Reserve would cut short-term interest rates—a move that ultimately materialized later in the quarter—alongside optimism that tariffs might prove less detrimental to smaller companies than initially feared. With the overall market moving higher and with a general “risk-on” sentiment, higher beta (more volatile), lower quality, and non-earning stocks performed much better in the quarter than their higher quality counterparts.

Fund Performance

The Virtus KAR Mid-Cap Core Fund returned 0.62% (Class I) in the quarter, underperforming the Russell Midcap® Index’s 5.33% return. Stock selection in the financials and industrials sectors detracted from performance. An underweight position in the consumer staples sector, combined with an overweight position and stock selection in the information technology sector, contributed positively to performance.

Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR) and Houlihan Lokey (HLI) were the largest contributors to performance during the quarter.

  • Monolithic Power, a provider of high-performance power solutions, saw strong broad-based revenue growth, which more than offset revenue declines in its Enterprise Data segment.
  • Global investment bank Houlihan Lokey benefited from robust mid-market mergers and acquisitions activity and steady demand for its restructuring advisory services, which translated into top-line and profit growth.

Align Technology (ALGN) and FactSet Research Systems (FDS) were the largest detractors from performance in the quarter.

  • Align Technology is a global medical device company that manufactures clear orthodontic aligners. Consumer sensitivity to interest rates has temporarily driven many orthodontia patients to brackets and wires, but fittings for aligners are up and market demand for the company’s products continues to be solid.
  • FactSet, a provider of financial data, market analysis, and insights, faced margin pressure stemming from elevated investment in AI and data infrastructure, while organic growth remained muted due to end-market headwinds facing asset managers.

Market Volatility: The value of the securities in the portfolio may go up or down in response to the prospects of individual companies and/or general economic conditions. Local, regional, or global events such as war or military conflict, terrorism, pandemic, or recession could impact the portfolio, including hampering the ability of the portfolio’s manager(s) to invest its assets as intended. data.title: Why Wealthfront Could Be The Costco Of Fintech
data.description:
Typically, I stay away from initial public offerings, or IPOs. It's a habit I picked up from Peter Lynch, who wrote that IPOs might as well have stood for "It's probably overpriced." Stocks can tend to do wacky things after an IPO, and I usually prefer to wait for hype to cool off and for more financial history to build up before evaluating and investing in a company.

But the latest fintech public offering was a company that I was a customer of, and I was itching to get a look at the stock's financials. That company is Wealthfront, which IPO'd earlier this month (December 12, 2025) at a price of $14 and has since dropped to $12.95, for a market cap of $1.89 billion at the time of writing.

Wealthfront Corporation (WLTH) is a robo-advisor that invests a customer's portfolio using a diversified, global, and low-tax ETF strategy. Wealthfront offers automatic tax-loss harvesting, where it will opportunistically sell an ETF at a loss and buy a similar ETF to harvest the tax loss while remaining invested. It also offers high-yield cash accounts and banking with basically the best interest rates I've come across, especially considering it has all the perks of a checking account. NerdWallet gives Wealthfront a 5 out of 5-star rating on its list of robo-advisors, and Wealthfront is perennially NerdWallet's top choice.

Wealthfront continues to expand its offerings, having rolled out features like an automated bond ladder and S&P 500 Direct, letting users invest directly in S&P 500 companies while excluding ones they would rather not invest in. They already have new features coming up, such as securities lending and fractional shares, according to a blog post. In particular, I think Wealthfront's automated tax-loss harvesting is one of its best features and in my experience, the savings it produces mean the 0.25% advisory fee pays for itself.

Wealthfront's business model revolves around doing well when customers do well, which is why I think Wealthfront could be the Costco of fintech. To review, Costco's business model is based around getting the best deals from suppliers and then passing those savings onto customers. Then they charge customers a subscription fee, which is basically pure profit.

With Wealthfront, they invest users in low-cost ETFs, and are constantly looking for ways to improve. The way Costco might switch suppliers to unlock savings for its customers, Wealthfront opportunistically changes the ETFs it users' investment portfolios in order to get lower expenses. They don't just do this for the sake of customer service; Wealthfront makes money when its users make money. By taking a percentage of assets under management, Wealthfront aligns itself with its users so that everyone is mutually invested in the customer building wealth.

Like Costco, Wealthfront doesn't spend much on marketing. For the six months ended July 31, 2025, product development made up $41 million of operating expenses, while marketing was $19 million, according to the financial data in the prospectus. And Wealthfront's marketing spend is unique. Wealthfront rewards users who sign up with referral links for both cash and investment accounts. For cash accounts, it's a 0.5% APY boost (so a cash account yielding 3.5% will instead earn 4% for a few months), and for investment accounts it is lower investment fees. In my opinion, Wealthfront's method of keeping marketing spend low so it can deliver more value to customers is Costco-esque.

It should already be clear that I have a lot of good things to say about Wealthfront as a product and as a business model. But being a good product doesn't always equate to being a good stock. Let's take a look at the numbers.

Valuation

Using the financial data from last year (the fiscal year ending January 31, 2025), gets us $117 million in free cash flow, with $210m in net cash (aka negative $210m in net debt), at a $1.89 billion market cap. Plugged into a reverse DCF calculator using a five-year forecast, a 3% terminal growth rate and a 10% weighted average cost of capital gives an implied FCF growth rate of 2.37%. At a WACC of 12% this increases to an implied growth rate of 8.51%, and at a WACC of 15% the implied growth rate is 16.43%.

$117 million is a conservative starting point for free cash flow, since Wealthfront has already generated $77 million in cash provided by operating activities in the six months ended July 31, 2025, against just $632 thousand in capital expenditures over that time.

Wealthfront is projecting YoY growth in Q3 as well. Revenue for the quarter is expected to be in the range of $91-95 million, for a 16% YoY increase at the midpoint of that range. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be $43-45 million, for a 24% YoY increase in the midpoint of that range.

Management

To quickly touch on management, David Fortunato is the CEO and has held the position since 2021. He was previously Wealthfront's Chief Technology Officer for 10 years, reinforcing that Wealthfront is first and foremost a technology company.

As of December 1, 2025, Fortunato owns just under 6% of the common shares outstanding, and all executive officers and directors own 29% combined, according to the 424B4 filing. Another large shareholder of note is cofounder Andrew Rachleff, who was CEO before Fortunato. His stake is 11%.

As is standard with IPOs, there is a lock-up period where insiders cannot sell shares. For Wealthfront, the lock-up period ends at the earlier of 180 days after the prospectus (which was filed December 12, 2025) or the second trading day after the earnings report for the quarter ending April 30, 2026. In other words, the lock-up period approximately ends in June 2026.

There is also a clause specifying that employees and a select couple of major investors can sell some shares if the stock price has multiple days above 125% of its IPO price, which is $17.5. I'm not much of a chartist, but my read on that is perhaps there will be "resistance" at $17.5 if the stock reaches that number in the next six months. Search for the "Lock-Up and Market Stand-Off Agreements" section of the prospectus if that is something that interests you.

Finally, a person of interest on Wealthfront's team is American economist Burton Malkiel, author of A Random Walk Down Wall Street, who serves as Wealthfront's Chief Investment Officer. A personal anecdote: when I was younger, after my father suggested Wealthfront as a place to put my money, I read A Random Walk Down Wall Street as one of my first books on investing. Halfway through, as Malkiel continued to make the case for the efficient-market hypothesis and diversification, I thought to myself that it sounded like Wealthfront was exactly what he would recommend. Sure enough, near the end of the book, he reveals his role at Wealthfront. It solidified Wealthfront's position in my mind as the premier place to park money for someone looking to take the "buy the entire market" approach to investing, and further proves to me that Wealthfront is coherent and firm in the way it has positioned itself in fintech.

Risks

I usually ignore macroeconomic factors when analyzing a stock, especially a small or mid-cap company, in favor of focusing on things like the business model, the balance sheet, and management. But Wealthfront has been and will continue to be directly affected by the Fed's interest rate decisions in a way that not many companies are.

Wealthfront adjusts its cash account APYs in lockstep with the Fed's lowering or raising of interest rates. Notice how the increase in platform assets coincided with the increase in interest rates from almost 0 through 2020-2022. Wealthfront's cash accounts went from offering a measly 0.25% APY to more like 4-5% when the Fed jacked up rates to fight inflation. Lower interest rates will make Wealthfront's cash account offering less enticing, and it looks like rates will either hold steady or go lower in the near future. Wealthfront cash accounts currently have a 3.5% yield.

Additionally, since Wealthfront's investment advisory segment takes a percentage fee of investment capital, Wealthfront will see revenues and profits shrink as investors' assets fall with a broader market fall.

Conclusion

I tried to write an objective analysis of Wealthfront, and ended up with something that reads at times like an advertisement. It's because I'm a big fan of Wealthfront as a product and business model. The problem is, consumers don't always have good taste. This isn't necessarily a fault of the customer, and you could chalk it up to a simple side effect of spending relatively little on marketing compared to on product development. But the point is that good product isn't guaranteed to win out.

The biggest risk for Wealthfront might be that people would rather speculate on prediction markets and 0DTE options instead of taking the slow and steady approach of diversified ETF investing. Perhaps Robinhood takes the share of retail investors looking for excitement and individual stock picking, and the steady investors choose to invest in their 401(k) accounts, leaving Wealthfront in an in-between fintech limbo.

It seems like a coherent argument to make, but I wouldn't bet on it. I think the market for people looking to invest online will continue to expand, and that Wealthfront's offering is so strong that users will willingly stick with Wealthfront and use it as their investment vehicle.

Wealthfront is priced for some growth, but not nearly enough in my opinion. The reasonable valuation as well as management's high ownership stake makes me comfortable opening a position here, and initiating my coverage of Wealthfront with a Buy.

data.title: A. O. Smith: Growth Re-Acceleration Supported By Strong Execution And Macro Recovery
data.description:
Investment Thesis

A. O. Smith Corporation's (AOS) revenue growth is poised to accelerate from FY26 as macro headwinds ease, and the company continues to execute well. The North America business should benefit from an interest rate reversal that should support a gradual recovery in housing activity. The company has been outperforming its end market and is gaining market share, which I expect to continue. With consumer focus on energy efficiency and total cost of ownership, the company’s commercial water heaters and boilers businesses should continue to see good growth. The U.S. Department of Energy’s new efficiency standards should also support this growth. In the Rest of the World segment, China remains a near-term headwind as consumer demand is weak, and government subsidies are reduced. However, the company is assessing go-to-market changes and potential adjustments to its business model and partnership opportunities. Further, the Indian market is a long-term revenue growth opportunity. Besides organic growth, the Leonard Valve acquisition should add to revenue growth.

The margins should benefit from pricing actions and cost control initiatives. Further, a mix shift toward higher-efficiency water heaters and boilers, and the exit from lower-margin retail water treatment channels, should drive margin expansion. The margins should also benefit from integration synergies from the Leonard Valve and Pureit acquisitions. In terms of valuation, the stock is trading at a discount to its historical averages. As sales and earnings growth start improving moving forward, I believe the stock can see some re-rating. So, I find risk/reward attractive at the current level, and hence rate AOS stock a Buy.

Recent Financial Performance

In Q3 2025, AOS reported sales of $943 mn, up 4% Y/Y. North America sales increased by 6% Y/Y driven by pricing increases and higher commercial water heater and boiler volumes. NA Boiler sales were up 10% Y/Y on higher pricing and increased volumes of high-efficiency boilers. NA water treatment sales were down 5% Y/Y as continued growth in the priority channels (dealer, e-commerce and direct-to-consumer) was more than offset by a decrease in the retail channel.

The Rest of World segment’s sales declined by 1% Y/Y. In China, continued economic challenges and reduced availability of government subsidy programs resulted in a 12% decline in local currency sales. However, sales in the legacy India business grew 13% in local currency. The Pureit acquisition contributed $17 mn of sales.

The North America segment operating margin expanded by 110 bps Y/Y to 24.2% attributed to pricing increases and higher water heater and boiler volumes. The Rest of World segment operating margin increased by 90 bps Y/Y to 7.4%. This was helped by the benefits from the restructuring initiatives that the company undertook in 2024 and cost-saving measures.

Outlook

A. O. Smith has faced tough macroeconomic conditions in 2025 with the slower U.S. residential market and tough conditions in China. However, the company has executed well, gaining market share, maintaining pricing discipline, and posting strong margin performance. Moving forward, as the interest rate cycle continues to reverse, and favorable regulatory and consumer trends toward higher-efficiency heating systems, I expect the company to see growth acceleration in FY26 and beyond.

North America contributes approximately three fourths of A. O. Smith’s total revenues. In recent years, a high-interest rate environment, a tough housing market especially multifamily weakness, and softer consumer confidence have impacted demand in this market. However, as the Federal Reserve continues to cut interest rates, I expect housing activity to rebound over the next few years. A. O. Smith has already been outperforming its end markets, and North American water heater sales was up 6% year over year in Q3 despite a decline in end-market demand. I expect continued market share gains and the company should emerge stronger as the housing recovery continues to take hold.

The commercial water heater market should remain solid as customers continue to focus on energy efficiency and total costs of ownership. With a strong position in high-efficiency condensing gas and heat pump commercial water heaters, the company is well positioned to benefit from this trend. The recently launched Flex commercial water heaters are enjoying good traction and aiding market share gains. Further from October 2026, the U.S. Department of Energy's new efficiency standards should further benefit A. O. Smith given its focus on energy-efficient offerings.

The boiler business, which makes around 8% of total sales, is also doing well. The segment is benefiting from price increases and mix improvement toward higher-efficiency products. The Lochinvar brand is benefiting from continued demand for energy-efficient offerings. Management has also noted stable quoting activity, especially for large commercial boilers.

Regarding the water treatment business, it is seeing some revenue pressure in the near term as the company is exiting low-margin retail channels. However, I am not too worried about it. This is a strategic decision and should benefit the company in the long run. Higher-margin channels like dealer, e-commerce, and direct-to-consumer continue to do well, and were up double digits in Q3. So, I believe the underlying business continues to remain strong in this segment.

In addition to organic growth, the company’s North America business should also benefit from the recently announced acquisition of Leonard Valve. This is a high-quality business with good pricing power and is mostly focused on repair-and-replacement demand, making it less cyclical. I expect good cross-selling synergies from this business.

Internationally, demand conditions are mixed. China remains pressured by low consumer demand, discontinuation of government subsidy programs, and competitive discounting. The company has decided against aggressive discounting of its premium brand positioning, which has helped margins but negatively impacted volumes. The company is doing a strategic assessment of its China business, evaluating go-to-market changes as well as potential adjustments to its business model and partnership opportunities. This includes expanding into broader categories that can be connected by smart home solutions, which can help drive sales. However, we are still in early stages and I believe China still remains a wait-and-see market for A. O. Smith.

In contrast, India remains a bright spot. The company delivered 13% Y/Y local currency growth in legacy business, and Pureit also added $17 mn in sales. I expect India to see continued double-digit growth over the next several years.

Overall, if we think about North American demand recovery with interest rate cycle reversal, the company continuing to take market share, efficiency standards providing further tailwinds, and India remaining strong, I believe A. O. Smith can deliver revenue acceleration in FY26 and beyond. China is still a wait-and-see market, but strength in other categories should support good growth.

On the margin front, A. O. Smith has demonstrated excellent execution, implementing price increases to more than offset both tariffs and other input costs inflation. This helped the company expand margin last quarter. In addition to pricing, management has also done a good job in terms of footprint optimization, strategic sourcing, and cost control. The company also level-loaded production last quarter to bring output closer to market demand. This helped it eliminate excess inventory and improved efficiency. I expect management to remain disciplined, and its cost control initiatives should continue to deliver margin improvement moving forward as well.

Mix improvement is another margin lever for North America. The company’s exit from lower-margin retail channels and focus on higher-margin dealer, e-commerce, and direct-to-consumer channels are expected to drive a 250-basis-point improvement in operating margin for the North American Water treatment business. The mix shift toward higher-efficiency products across its businesses should also support margins. The Leonard Valve acquisition, which has relatively higher margins compared to A. O. Smith, should also help, and the company should benefit from margin synergies as it integrates it.

Internationally, the company continues to maintain pricing and cost discipline, which should help it expand margins moving forward as well. It should also benefit from integration synergy with Pureit in the long run.

So, I expect A. O. Smith to see good margin expansion moving forward as well. This, coupled with growth re-acceleration with end-market recovery driven by interest rate cycle reversal, improved demand for high-efficiency products, and continued market share gains, should support good EPS growth.

Valuation

A. O. Smith currently trades at ~16.4x FY26 consensus EPS estimate of $4.13, which is approximately 20% discount to its five-year historical average forward P/E multiple of approximately 20.6x at these levels.

I believe slower growth in China is already getting priced in the valuation. A. O. Smith is executing well in the rest of the business in North America and internationally, ex-China. The strength in these markets should more than offset the weakness in China. As the company continues to increase market share, keep price discipline, and benefit from a mix shift towards higher efficiency products, it can see good growth moving forward.

In addition, macros turning positive should also help the growth, with interest rate cuts likely to revive housing demand. The recent Leonard Valve acquisition also adds a high-quality business. Further, the company was able to improve margin in its international business despite volume headwind in China. I believe investors are not giving enough credit to the company for its good execution on the margins front.

As sales and earnings growth start improving moving forward, I believe the stock can see some re-rating. Even without any meaningful re-rating, high single-digit EPS growth coupled with a 2% dividend yield should translate into high single-digit, low double-digit returns for the stock. I find risk/reward attractive at the current level, and hence rate AOS stock a Buy.

Risks

My thesis anticipates the North America business to benefit from the interest rate cuts. However, if the interest rate cuts are delayed or are less than anticipated, it could impact the business's performance.

China represents another risk. Prolonged consumer demand weakness, more competitive discounting, or unfavorable results from restructuring could further impact volumes and delay its recovery.

Another area to monitor will be the inorganic growth through acquisitions. If the company fails to recognize the intended synergies from the Leonard Valve and Pureit acquisitions, its stock price performance can be affected.
